overview: Activity on bpf-next for this period was dominated by a single 11-patch series (v3) from Kuniyuki Iwashima adding SOCK_OPS hooks for TCP AutoLOWAT. The series introduces BPF_SOCK_OPS_RCVQ_CB, a new callback that fires when the TCP receive queue changes, along with a kfunc to dynamically adjust sk_rcvlowat from BPF programs. This enables transparent, per-connection receive buffer tuning without any application or kernel changes.

patches.title: bpf: tcp: Introduce BPF_SOCK_OPS_RCVQ_CB.
patches.author: Kuniyuki Iwashima
patches.summary: Introduces BPF_SOCK_OPS_RCVQ_CB, a new sock_ops callback that fires whenever the TCP receive queue size changes on an established connection. BPF programs attached to this hook can observe receive queue growth events and react accordingly. This is the foundational patch of the TCP AutoLOWAT series, enabling BPF-driven receive low-water mark tuning. The callback opens the door to protocol-aware wakeup scheduling without any changes to userspace applications.

patches.title: bpf: tcp: Support bpf_skb_load_bytes() for BPF_SOCK_OPS_RCVQ_CB.
patches.author: Kuniyuki Iwashima
patches.summary: Extends BPF_SOCK_OPS_RCVQ_CB with the ability to call bpf_skb_load_bytes() to peek at data in the TCP receive queue from within the hook. This allows BPF logic to make rcvlowat decisions based on actual packet content and message framing rather than just queue depth alone. Protocol-aware tuning — for example, waiting until a complete message header is present — becomes straightforward with this helper access.

patches.title: tcp: Split out __tcp_set_rcvlowat().
patches.author: Kuniyuki Iwashima
patches.summary: Refactors the receive low-water mark assignment logic into a shared __tcp_set_rcvlowat() helper. This preparatory change lets both the existing setsockopt code path and the new BPF kfunc reuse the same validated logic for capping and applying sk_rcvlowat. Centralising the implementation prevents divergence and ensures consistent behavior when the threshold is adjusted from either context.

patches.title: bpf: tcp: Add kfunc to adjust sk->sk_rcvlowat.
patches.author: Kuniyuki Iwashima
patches.summary: Adds a kfunc that BPF programs can call from BPF_SOCK_OPS_RCVQ_CB to adjust the TCP socket's sk_rcvlowat threshold at runtime. This is the core mechanism behind TCP AutoLOWAT: BPF logic inspects the receive queue via bpf_skb_load_bytes() and then raises or lowers the low-water mark to suppress spurious application wakeups. The kfunc is restricted to the receive-queue callback context to prevent misuse. Combined with BPF_SOCK_OPS_RCVQ_CB, it delivers fully automated per-connection receive tuning.

patches.title: bpf: tcp: Make BPF_SOCK_OPS_RCVQ_CB and SOCKMAP mutually exclusive.
patches.author: Kuniyuki Iwashima
patches.summary: Adds a guard that rejects enabling BPF_SOCK_OPS_RCVQ_CB on sockets already enrolled in a SOCKMAP. The two features conflict because SOCKMAP intercepts the receive queue before the new callback would fire, making the callback semantics undefined in that configuration. This patch establishes a clear boundary between the two BPF socket interception mechanisms and prevents silent misbehavior.

patches.title: bpf: mptcp: Don't support BPF_SOCK_OPS_RCVQ_CB.
patches.author: Kuniyuki Iwashima
patches.summary: Explicitly disables BPF_SOCK_OPS_RCVQ_CB for MPTCP sockets because MPTCP's receive queue semantics differ significantly from plain TCP. Allowing the callback on MPTCP connections would require separate, more complex handling that is out of scope for this series. The patch returns an appropriate error if the callback is requested on an MPTCP socket, providing a clear failure mode rather than silent incorrect behavior.

patches.title: selftest: bpf: Add test for BPF_SOCK_OPS_RCVQ_CB.
patches.author: Kuniyuki Iwashima
patches.summary: Adds an end-to-end selftest that exercises the complete BPF_SOCK_OPS_RCVQ_CB and rcvlowat kfunc flow over a loopback TCP connection. The test verifies that the BPF program is invoked on receive queue events and that dynamically adjusting sk_rcvlowat via the kfunc correctly gates application wakeups. It serves as the integration test for the entire TCP AutoLOWAT feature series and guards against regressions.
